# Figure description: 34.jpg

**Source**: `36572_Smolianova_1984/raw_graphics/M-42-II/34.jpg`  
**Generated by**: claude-sonnet-4-6

---

## Figure Type

This is a **mineral resources map** (*Карта полезных ископаемых*) — a specialized thematic geological map showing the distribution of known and inferred mineral deposits, occurrences, and mineralization zones across a defined survey area. It is a standard deliverable in Soviet regional geological survey methodology (съёмка масштаба 1:200,000).

---

## Coverage and Subject

- **Map sheet:** M-42-II (noted prominently at top center), with reference to adjacent sheet M-42-XXXI visible in smaller text below
- **Scale:** 1:200,000 (stated explicitly: "Масштаб 1:200,000"; confirmed by scale bar: "В 1 сантиметре 2 километра")
- **Year:** 1984 г. (1984)
- **Geographic region:** Northern Kazakhstan (Казахская ССР / Kazakh SSR), covered by the Central Kazakhstan Production Geological Association (*Центрально-Казахстанское производственное геологическое объединение*)
- **Inset map:** Upper-left corner contains a smaller inset map at a different scale, covering a sub-area with elevation markers (e.g., -337, -448, -356, -392) and settlement references including what appears to be **ст. Красновосточная**, **пос. Загурьевка**, and **ст. Джексон** (or similar romanized transliterations) — likely showing depth-to-basement or an index of adjacent sheets
- **Named localities visible on main map:** Several small settlements and geographic names are annotated across the map body, including references to stations (*ст.*) and villages (*пос.*), though individual labels are difficult to resolve at this reproduction scale

---

## Key Content

### Geological Background (Base Map)
The map uses a standard Soviet 1:200,000 geological base with **stratigraphic-age color coding**:

- **Pale yellow / cream (dotted pattern):** Dominates the western and central portions — likely **Quaternary and Neogene sedimentary cover** (continental deposits, alluvium, eluvium). The dot-fill pattern is consistent with Soviet cartographic convention for loose Cenozoic sediments.
- **Orange-pink / salmon tones:** Scattered across the central and eastern areas — typically indicating **Paleozoic granitoid intrusions** (granites, granodiorites), consistent with the Central Kazakhstan Hercynian fold belt.
- **Brown / dark red-brown patches:** Concentrated in the south-central zone — may represent **Lower Paleozoic or Proterozoic metamorphic basement rocks**, or differentiated intrusive phases (possibly gabbro, diorite).
- **Green patches:** Scattered across multiple areas — in Soviet geological maps at this scale, green conventionally marks **volcanic sequences** (effusives), possibly Devonian or Carboniferous volcanics, or locally **serpentinized ultramafics**.
- **Gray-blue/lilac tones:** Present in parts of the eastern sector — may denote **sedimentary Paleozoic sequences** (carbonates, clastic sequences).
- **Linear structural features:** Multiple **fault lines** (solid and dashed) traverse the map, with a pronounced structural grain visible, particularly a series of **NW-SE trending lineaments** in the central portion converging toward what appears to be a structural node or intrusive complex.

### Mineralization and Deposit Symbols
Numerous **point symbols, numbered circles, squares, and color-coded mineral occurrence markers** are distributed across the map — standard Soviet cartographic notation for:
- Known deposits (*месторождения*)
- Mineral occurrences (*проявления*)
- Geochemical anomalies

These symbols appear densest in the **central structural zone**, correlating with the intrusive complex, and along major fault intersections — consistent with hydrothermal mineralization controlled by structural and lithological contacts.

### Structural Pattern
A **radial/concentric structural pattern** is visible in the central map area, suggesting a **domal or ring structure** — possibly a large plutonic complex with aureole contact metamorphism, which is a common host for polymetallic, skarn-type, or porphyry-style mineralization in the Central Kazakhstan metallogenic province.

## Research Value

This mineral resources map provides a **synoptic view of the known mineral endowment and geological framework** of map sheet M-42-II at 1:200,000 scale, compiled as a key deliverable of the 1983–1984 regional survey. A geologist would use it to identify **spatial relationships between mineralization occurrences, intrusive bodies, and fault structures**, which is essential for targeting
